3A_Bibliographic.citation: Yang, G., Li, Y., Liu, H., & Jiang, Q. (2024). Causal relationships between screen use, reading, and brain development in early adolescents. Advanced Science, 11(2), 2307540.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Uses Mendelian randomization approach for causal inference.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly stated; baseline and 2-year follow-up data used
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Table 1 shows the sociodemographic characteristics of the subjects included in the baseline (n = 7107) and 2-year follow-up (n = 4505) ABCD datasets, respectively.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Baseline n=7107; 2yr follow-up n=4505. Ns vary by analysis.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Six types of screen use activities: TVM (TV/movies), Video, Game, Text, SNS (social networking sites), VC (video chatting); and reading time
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networking sites, video chatting
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The study investigated six screen use activities: 1) watching television shows or movies (TVM), 2) watching videos (Video), 3) playing video games (Game), 4) texting on a cell phone, tablet, or computer (Text), 5) visiting social networking sites (SNS), and 6) video chatting (VC).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Cognitive scores (NIH Toolbox: picture vocabulary, oral reading recognition, picture sequence memory, flanker, dimensional card sort) and CBCL behavioral outcomes (8 subscales) and brain volume (160 regions)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: NIH Cognitive Toolbox, Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), FreeSurfer-based brain volumetrics from 3D T1-weighted MRI
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: We used a linear mixed-effect (LME) model to analyze the relationship between screen use and outcomes including 5 cognitive scores from the NIH cognitive toolbox and 8 behavioral outcomes from the child behavior checklist (CBCL) questionnaire at two time points.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains: cognition, behavior (mental health), and brain. Primary domain coded as cognition given the emphasis on language/cognitive causal effects.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear mixed-effects models for associations; one-sample and two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) for causal inference; mediation analysis for indirect effects via reading
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race, ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site (random effect)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used a linear mixed-effect (LME) model to analyze the relationship between screen use and outcomes. We focused on results that were significant at both time points for reliability consideration. Based on the above regression results, we performed MR analysis to determine the causal influence of screen use and reading habits on cognitions and behaviors.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Uses Mendelian randomization as primary causal inference method. LME for associations. Bonferroni and FDR correction applied.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: MR causal effect: change in cognitive score per hour of daily screen use
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: We found a negative causal effect of TVM use on picture vocabulary (beta = -2.39, corrected p < 0.02)
video use on withdrawn/depressed symptoms (beta = 0.57, corrected p < 0.008)
positive causal effect of reading on picture vocabulary (beta = 6.31, corrected p < 10^-14)
TVM use was negatively associated with two crystallized intelligence scores including picture vocabulary (beta = -1.07 and -1.09 at two time points, p < 10^-6)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Video use (MR causal on reading time)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: MR: change in daily reading hours per hour of video use

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: SNS use (MR causal on reading time)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: MR: change in daily reading hours per hour of SNS use

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: VC use (MR causal on reading time)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: MR: change in daily reading hours per hour of VC use

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: it is important to avoid exaggerating the negative consequences of screen use, as some behavioral problems may precede and contribute to excessive screen use.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper explicitly cautions against overstating negative effects of screen use. The MR approach provides causal evidence. The paper notes reverse causality (behavioral problems cause screen use) for some outcomes.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Scientific instruction like the Canadian 24-Hour movement guidelines should be proposed to regulate children's use of new media, as well as to ensure children's time in other activities such as reading, physical activity, and sleep. Taking proactive measures to promote the healthy development of young minds in the digital age is crucial.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Taking proactive measures to promote the healthy development of young minds in the digital age is crucial.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: The effect coefficients show the change in children's cognitive or behavioral scores due to daily screen time or reading per hour
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion takes a balanced approach, noting that MR analyses show screen use (especially TVM) has causal adverse effects on language abilities, but also finds that some behavioral problems (rule-breaking, aggression) are causes rather than consequences of screen use. It highlights an indirect pathway where screen use displaces reading, which in turn harms cognition and brain volume. The paper explicitly cautions against overstating screen use effects and acknowledges bidirectional relationships.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: MR effect sizes from one-sample analysis are difficult to interpret in clinically meaningful units. Two-sample MR validation uses adult UK Biobank data which may not generalize to adolescents. CIs not reported for all MR effects in main text.
